text:
We are into the final few days 2024 Paris Olympics, but there are still plenty of medal hopes left for Team GB. Leah Crisp is already underway this morning in the 10km open-water swim in the River Seine, the event going ahead despite further concerns over the water.

Katarina Johnson-Thompson gets her heptathlon medal bid underway today, and more track and field drama is guaranteed in the men's 200m, women's 400m and men's 110m hurdles.

Team GB currently lie fifth in the medal table with an overall total of 49, with 12 of them being Gold. Matthew Hudson-Smith's silver in the 400m last night took the British total to 1000 medals since the Summer Games began in 1896.

You can follow all the latest Olympics news in our LIVE blog, below....

That brings the curtain down on another busy day of Olympics action.

Ellie Aldridge took home the only gold for Team GB to make it a lucky for 13 for the 2024 Games in Paris. Will they add to that total tomorrow? Join us to find out.

Until then, goodnight!

USA athlete Noah Lyles has explained why he ran the men's 200m final despite being diagnosed withCovidthree days ago.

The 27-year-old finished with a bronze medal but looked strangely off colour as Botswana's Letsile Tebogo took the gold, revealing after that he had contracted the illness.

“I do have Covid. I tested positive around 5am on Tuesday morning. I woke up in the middle of the night feeling real chills, aching, sore throat, and those were a lot of the symptoms I’ve had right before getting Covid," Lyles said.

"We tested, it came back positive and we quickly quarantined in a hotel nearby the [Olympic] Village. We tried to get me on as much medication as we legally could to make sure my body could keep the momentum going.

"I still wanted to run. It was still possible - we just stayed away from everybody. I knew that if I wanted to come out here and win. I had to give everything I had from the get go. I didn’t have any time to save energy. That was the strategy."

Grant Holloway has finally clinched the Olympic gold medal that had eluded him in the 110m hurdles final.

The American celebrated beating his USA team-mate Daniel Roberts with a time of 12.99 despite nearly stumbling over at the line.

Jamaica's Rasheed Broadbell took the final bronze position but there was no real challenge to Holloway's blistering pace.



It's a world record in the 400m hurdles!

No one could get near Sydney McLaughlin-Levrone as she stormed home to smash the world record with a time of 50.37 - half a second quicker than the previous record.

The USA star finished ahead of her compatriot Anna Cockrell, with Femke Bol taking bronze.

A remarkable fun from the 25-year-old, who was only just outside the times for the 400m final - and that's without hurdles!

Katrina Johnson-Thompson was fastest in the women's 200m event of the heptathlon.

The Team GB star collected 1,035 points from her time of 23.44, putting her at the top of the standings with 4,055 overall.

That takes her above Belgium's Nafi Thiam in the standings.

Noah Lyles has been diagnosed with Covid just minutes after finishing with a bronze medal in the 200m final.

The American came third in the race behind his USA team-mate Kenny Bednarek and gold medal winner Letsile Tebogo, before he was taken off the track in a wheelchair.

Lyles had been spotted wearing a PPE mask in the call room before the race.

And it's now been confirmed by Team USA that Lyles has contracted Covid.

Letsile Tebogo is the new 200m champion.

The younger has won the men's 200m final to claim a historic first gold medal for Botswana.

The 21-year-old, who dedicated his efforts in Paris to his late mother, was streets ahead of USA pair Kenny Bednarek and Noah Lyles.

He ran a time of 20.45 seconds, just shy of the Olympic record.



Team GB athlete Bradly Sinden has been denied a chance to compete for the bronze medal in the men's taekwondo -68kg event at the Paris 2024 Olympics.

The Briton had been due to face China's Yushuai Liang on Thursday night after he lost to Jordan's Zaid Kareen earlier in the day.

But the 25-year-old has been forced to withdraw from the fight after he was seen limping during that particular clash, with the injury deemed to be too painful for him to participate.

A Team GB statement read: "Due to an injury sustained in his quarter-final earlier today, Bradly Sinden is sadly unable to compete for bronze in the men’s -68kg taekwondo competition this evening."

READ MORE

Team GB Emma Finucane believes her bronze medal feels like a gold after summoning all of her strength to finish in the top three of the women's keirin final.

“Going up in that final and scraping through the semi, I knew I had to find something in my legs that I hadn't seen before," she toldBBCOne.

"Ellesse [Andrews] is world champion, she has demonstrated today that she is so strong and all of the other girls in that final, we’re the strongest girls in the world that’s why we are in the Olympic final and to even be there next to one of my best friends Katie [Marchant] was such a pinch me moment.

“To get a bronze medal, it literally feels like gold to me because I left everything out there on the track.

“If you told me a year ago I’d be coming to the Olympics and getting a gold in track sprint and then bronze in keirin, I would've been like ‘no way’ but here I am.”



Team GB's Jack Carlin edges a thrilling battle with Japan's Kaiya Ota to make it through to the men's sprint semi-final.

However, the 27-year-old could be kicked out after a potential infringement when racing feistily against Ota.

Sir Chris Hoy says he should be "concerned" about potentially being relegated by officials after crossing the red line during the race.

Elsewhere, Britain's Hamish Turnbull won the first of three races against Jeffrey Hoogland, but the Dutch rider then took the next two to advance into the semi-finals.

Emma Finucane came close in the women's keirin final but the Team GB cyclist has to settle for bronze.

It's her second medal at the Games and she'll be pleased with her performance there.

She finished third in her semi-final race and was pipped to silver by the Netherlands rider Hetty van der Wouw. Ellesse Andrews of New Zealand took the gold.

Sadly, her compatriot Katy Marchant missed out on a top-three finish.

Ethan Hayter has redeemed himself after claiming victory in the third points race in the omnium.

He pumps his fist as he takes maximum points with victory in front of a large British section of the crowd.

Hayter was disappointed after going too hard in the team event on Wednesday, but he's recovered to put himself fifth going into the final event, the points race.

A top-three finish there and he could be walking away with a medal.

Britain's Emma Finucane has made it through to the women's keirin final after finishing third in her semi-final heat.

It went to a photo finish between Britain's Finucane and Steffie van der Peet of the Netherlands to see who got third.

But it went her way with a tiny 0.004 margin separating the two riders, with Finucane finishing 0.157 behind the fastest cyclist.

Katy Marchant made it through to the final of the sprint after qualifying second.

Ellie Aldridge has done it!

The Briton has clinched gold in the kiting event ahead of the Netherlands and USA.

It's been a long time coming, but that's the lucky 13th gold medal of the Olympics for Great Britain and Northern Ireland.

The 27-year-old celebrates on the podium with the national anthem ringing out.



Jack Laugher's quest for a fifth Olympic medal ended in disappointment as he finished seventh in the men's diving 3m springboard final.

A mistake on his third dive left the Briton with too much to do and he even finished below his compatriot Jordan Holden, who came in fifth.

"I let emotion get the better of me," the Team GB diver told BBC Sport.

China's Xie Siyi produced a phenonemal dive on his final attempt to secure the gold medal ahead of Wang Zongyuan.

He won it with 543.60, 13 points clear of his team-mate, with Mexico's Osmar Olvera taking the bronze.

Simone Biles' coach described the decision to penalise the Team USA athlete as "a little harsh" after a minor transgression in her balance beam routine.

Biles came fifth in the balance beam due to a rare tumble in the event, but it emerged that her score of 13.100 reflected a 0.3-point deduction for failing to conclude her routine properly.

Coach Cecile Landi questioned the decision along with many disappointed American gymnastics fans, although she insisted they would move on from the disappointment.

"We watched it," she told reporters as quoted by USA Today. "I could see it, yes and no. I think it's a little harsh, but at the end, it didn't matter. So no, we're not going to make a big deal out of it."

Taiwan's Lin Yu-ting has hit out at the International Boxing Association for their 'ridiculous' attempts to ban her from competition after securing an Olympic bronze medal.

The boxer has been at the centre of a gender row in Paris over a previously undisclosed gender test, facing false claims that she is biologically male.

The International Olympic Committee (IOC) cleared her to compete in the 2024 Games as their eligibility rules are different.

“Of course it annoys you when reading some remarks, but all we can do is tell ourselves we can't control what they say,” Lin told the Chinese News Agency.

“Let them talk the talk, but I have a clear conscience on this. After all, we have proven it false [the IBA's test], and the process didn't follow any standards, but there was still a fuss. I wonder if they were deliberately going after me. I was confused. It was kind of ridiculous."



Great Britain's Bradly Sinden has moved a step closer to a guaranteed silver medal after defeating Croatia's Marko Golubic.

Doncaster native Sinden beat Golubic in the deciding round of their tie to make it through to the -68kg semi-finals.

If he wins that, he will guarantee himself a silver medal in Paris. But this time, he will be going for gold after missing out in Tokyo three years ago.

Jordan Houlden is fifth and Jack Laugher seventh after three rounds of the men's 3m springboard diving final.

Chinese diver Wang Zongyuan is top on 268.15, five points clear of compatriot Xie Siyi.

Medal hopes are fading for the British pair.

BBCcommentator Steve Backley was corrected live on air after failing to use the preferred pronouns for shot putter Raven Saunders.

Backley, a formerOlympicjavelin silver medalist, repeatedly described Saunders as 'her' during coverage . Co-host Jazmin Sawyers pointed out that the star identifies as non-binary, and uses different pronouns.

READ MORE HERE.



Switzerland's Morgane Metraux is the new leader at Le Golf National after a superb second round 66.

That leaves her on -8, currently two clear of Australia's Lydia Ko.

Georgia Hall leads the British challenge but at +3, is a long way off a medal.

Team GB's Katarina Johnson-Thompson leads after two events of the heptathlon after clearing 1.92 metres in the high jump.

She's on 2197 points, with Nafissatou Thiam of Belgium second on 2173.

It's been a terrible hour for Team GB.

After disappointment for Jade Jones, John Gimson and Anna Burnet have been disqualified in the mixed multi-hull sailing medal race.

They were judged to have been over the line when the starting horn sounded, and were pulled out of the double-points race.

FULL STORY HERE.

Heartbreak for Jade Jones.

There will be no third Olympic gold as she's been beaten by North Macedonia's Miljana Reljikj.

The pair were level after two rounds, but Reljikj took the decider to cause a huge shock.



Bradly Sinden was superb there in his 12-0 15-3 win over Papua New Guinea's Kevin Kassman.

He's now into the quarter-final stage of the men's 68kg taekwondo. And he looks a genuine medal hope.

To say Morgane Metraux is charging is an understatement!

She's picked up six shots in the first six holes of her second round to move to -8. Home favourite Celine Boutier is currently the joint leader with the Swiss player.

Georgia Hall has opened up with two pars to stay +2.

A very good start for Katarina Johnson-Thompson in the heptathlon.

She was second in he rheat of the 100m hurdles in a season's best time of 13.40, behind Poland's Adriana Sulek-Schubert in 13.32.

Jade O'Dowda was third 13.53.

That was brutal!

Bu it's gold for the Netherlands' Sharon van Rouwendaal. Australia's Moesha Johnson won silver and Italy's Ginevra Taddeucci bronze.

Great Britain's Leah Crisp, who was in the leading pack for much of the early going, faded and finished in 20th.



The second round of the women's golf is underway at Le Golf National.

France's Celine Boutier has a three-shot lead after a seven-under 65 in the first round, a performance which delighted the French crowd.

Georgia Hall starts her second round at 09:22am and is +2, but Charley Hull has a mountain to climb after an 81 left her +9. She tees off at 10:44am.

American Kenny Bednarek has hit out ahead of the 200m men’s final after accusing organisers of favouring rival Noah Lyles.

Despite winning his heat, Bednarek was put in lane 9 for the final while Lyles got lane 5.

After a protest he's since been put in lane 8, but wrote on X: "Never seen favoritism like this on a global scale."

Never seen favoritism like this on a global scale.

Leah Crisp is struggling to keep the pace now.

She's fallen right back, and is 39 seconds off Australian leader Moesha Johnson. The Italian due of Ginevra Taddeucci and Giulia Gabbrielleschi are second and third.

We use your sign-up to provide content in ways you've consented to and to improve our understanding of you. This may include adverts from us and 3rd parties based on our understanding. You can unsubscribe at any time. Read ourPrivacy Policy

Ethiopa's Lamecha Girma has reportedly regained consciousness after his heavy fall in men's steeplechase 3,000m steeplechase.

Girma, 23, the current world record holder, was stretchered off of and rushed to hosptial after passing out on the track.

According toL'Equipe, his coach Teshome Kebede has said Girma is now "in good condition."

Coach Teshome Kebede said "Lamecha Girma is in good condition.\u201dpic.twitter.com/EUDb06pc65

Welcome back to another day of live coverage from the Olympics!

And a Team GB medal hope is already in action, with Leah Crisp in the leading pack so far in the women's 10km marathon swim in the River Seine.

title: Olympics news: Gender row boxer speaks out, Simone Biles' coach blasts judges | Other | Sport | Express.co.uk
author: Joe Krishnan, Fraser Watson
description: Express Sport recaps all the latest Paris Olympics news from day 13 of the Games.
